ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKM (Rice dishes) is the closest defensible category. No specific FoodEx2 code exists for soft shell turtle rice combination dishes.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Xiaoxiang Soft Shell Turtle with Wuchang Rice is approximately 350g, comprising a bowl of premium Wuchang rice topped with braised soft shell turtle pieces and Chinese herbal broth.

nutrient_source.title: Xiaoxiang Soft Shell Turtle With Wuchang Rice — HPB Singapore Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this dish (cr_id G00010913). All per_100g macros and micronutrients are taken from the HPB record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is not reported in the HPB record; estimated at 1.1g/100g based on USDA FoodData Central data for soft shell turtle (USDA FDC ID 174199) and cooked white rice (USDA FDC ID 168878) in approximate dish proportions. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several micronutrients not reported by HPB are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice (short-grain, cooked)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific dish. GI estimated at 64 (medium) based on the dominant carbohydrate component: Wuchang rice is a premium short-grain japonica white rice. The University of Sydney GI Database reports short-grain white rice (cooked) at GI ~64–72. A conservative mid-range value of 64 is applied, moderated downward by the presence of dietary fibre (3.7g/100g), protein (6.5g/100g), and fat (4.21g/100g) from the turtle and herb components, which attenuate glycaemic response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × total carbohydrate per 100g × serving_g / 100) / 100 = (64 × 25.19 × 350 / 100) / 100 = 64 × 88.165 / 100 = 56.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231mg/100g; partially offset by high potassium 327mg/100g (K:Na ratio ~1.4:1)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce in the braising liquid and monitor total meal sodium from accompaniments, as the dish provides 231mg sodium per 100g; the high potassium content (327mg/100g) from turtle meat and Chinese herbs (wolfberry, red dates) provides a favourable offset, but a 350g serving still delivers approximately 809mg sodium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 25.19g/100g from short-grain Wuchang rice with estimated GI ~64 (medium); dietary fibre 3.7g/100g provides partial attenuation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should request a smaller rice portion and a larger turtle and herb component to reduce glycaemic load, as a standard 350g serving delivers approximately 88g total carbohydrate with an estimated glycaemic load of 56; pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables and choosing brown rice as a substitute would further moderate postprandial blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 4.21g/100g (moderate); cholesterol reported as 0mg/100g by HPB; high potassium and iron; soft shell turtle provides collagen and omega-3 fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish presents a relatively low cardiovascular risk profile given its moderate total fat (4.21g/100g), high potassium (327mg/100g supporting vascular function), and the anti-inflammatory properties of soft shell turtle collagen and omega-3 fatty acids; however, individuals with cardiovascular disease should monitor sodium intake (231mg/100g) and request reduced soy sauce to keep total meal sodium within recommended limits.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory components: soft shell turtle omega-3 and collagen; ginger gingerols; wolfberry and red date antioxidants
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Soft shell turtle provides omega-3 fatty acids and collagen with anti-inflammatory properties, and the Chinese herbs (wolfberry, red dates, ginger, garlic) contribute antioxidants and gingerols that may support joint health; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis on medication should monitor their response to Chinese herbs and request reduced soy sauce to keep sodium below 400mg/100g.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Xiaoxiang Soft Shell Turtle with Wuchang Rice (潇满湘甲鱼配五常大米) is a premium Chinese restaurant dish originating from Hunan-style cuisine, served in Singapore. The dish features braised soft shell turtle (甲鱼, jiǎyú) — a prized ingredient in traditional Chinese medicine and gastronomy — slow-cooked with soy sauce, cooking wine, ginger, garlic, wolfberry (goji berry), red dates, and Chinese herbs, then served over or alongside Wuchang rice (五常大米), a premium short-grain japonica rice from Wuchang, Heilongjiang Province, China, renowned for its fragrant aroma, slightly sticky texture, and superior flavour. The turtle meat is tender, gelatinous, and rich in collagen, with a mild umami flavour from the herbal braising liquid. The Wuchang rice is soft, slightly sticky, and and subtly fragrant. Nutritionally, the dish provides 164 kcal per 100g, with 6.5g protein, 4.21g fat, and 25.19g carbohydrate. Dietary fibre is notable at 3.7g/100g, and potassium is high at 327mg/100g. Sodium is 231mg/100g, primarily from soy sauce in the braising liquid. The dish is clinically relevant for individuals managing hypertension (moderate sodium, high potassium offset), Type 2 Diabetes (medium GI rice base, fibre moderation), and cardiovascular disease (low fat, anti-inflammatory turtle components). Soft shell turtle is a traditional tonic food in Chinese medicine, believed to nourish yin, strengthen the kidneys, and reduce internal heat. The dish is typically served at lunch or dinner in Chinese restaurants in Singapore.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010913) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous components. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database for short-grain white rice as the dominant carbohydrate source. All mandatory multilingual names provided.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.1g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for soft shell turtle (FDC ID 174199) and cooked short-grain white rice (FDC ID 168878) in approximate dish proportions; not directly reported by HPB. 2. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown not available in HPB Singapore Foodo database for this dish as of 2026-04-16. 3. Vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this dish; USDA FoodData Central does not have a direct composite entry for this specific dish combination. 4. Cholesterol is reported as 0.0mg/100g by HPB; this is noted as potentially underestimated given that soft shell turtle meat typically contains cholesterol; however, the HPB value is preserved as the primary approved source. 5. GI of 64 is an analogous estimate based on short-grain white rice from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement exists for this specific dish. Glycemic load per 350g serving = 56.4 (using total carbohydrate). 6. The dish is not certified halal as soft shell turtle (甲鱼) is not halal-certified in Singapore. 7. Dietary fibre at 3.7g/100g is notably high for a rice-based dish, reflecting the contribution of Chinese herbs, wolfberry, and red dates in the braising components.
